title: Core Concepts in Artificial Intelligence
body:
Understanding modern AI requires grasping a handful of foundational concepts that underpin virtually everything the field produces today. The most fundamental distinction is between the three primary learning paradigms. Supervised learning is the workhorse: you provide the model with labelled examples — thousands of images tagged as 'cat' or 'dog', millions of sentences paired with their translations — and the model learns a function that maps inputs to outputs. This is how spam filters, image classifiers, and medical diagnosis systems are typically built. Unsupervised learning, by contrast, works with unlabelled data and seeks to discover structure — clustering similar documents, reducing high-dimensional data to manageable representations, or learning the statistical distribution of a dataset so well that the model can generate new samples. Reinforcement learning takes a different approach entirely: an agent interacts with an environment, receives rewards or penalties for its actions, and learns a policy that maximises cumulative reward over time. This paradigm produced AlphaGo, trained robotic manipulation skills, and is now central to making language models more helpful through techniques like RLHF.

The transformer architecture deserves special attention because it is the engine behind the current AI revolution. Introduced in the 2017 paper 'Attention Is All You Need,' transformers replaced the recurrent neural networks that had previously dominated sequence processing. The key innovation is the attention mechanism — a mathematical operation that allows each element in a sequence to attend to every other element, computing relevance scores that determine how much influence each part of the input has on the processing of each other part. This parallel processing capability, unlike the sequential nature of recurrent networks, enabled transformers to scale efficiently to massive sizes and massive datasets. GPT, BERT, Claude, Gemini, and virtually every frontier AI system is built on transformer architectures or close derivatives.

Embeddings are another concept that pervades modern AI. An embedding is a learned numerical representation — a vector of numbers — that captures the meaning or characteristics of an input in a format that neural networks can work with. Words, sentences, images, audio clips, and even code can all be converted into embeddings. The power of embeddings is that similar things end up close together in the embedding space: the embeddings for 'king' and 'queen' are closer to each other than either is to 'bicycle.' This property enables semantic search, recommendation systems, clustering, and the kind of flexible pattern matching that makes modern AI systems feel surprisingly intelligent.

Training and inference are the two fundamental phases of working with AI models, and understanding the distinction matters. Training is the computationally expensive process of adjusting a model's parameters using data — it can take weeks on clusters of thousands of GPUs and cost millions of dollars for frontier models. Inference is the comparatively cheap process of using a trained model to make predictions on new inputs. Fine-tuning sits between the two: taking a pre-trained model and continuing its training on a smaller, domain-specific dataset to specialise its capabilities. This transfer learning approach is what makes foundation models so powerful — a single expensive pre-training run produces a general-purpose model that can be fine-tuned cheaply for thousands of specific applications.

Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) is the technique that transformed large language models from impressive but unreliable text generators into the helpful, conversational systems deployed today. The process works in stages: first, a language model is pre-trained on a massive text corpus to learn language patterns. Then, human evaluators rank different model responses to the same prompt, and a separate reward model is trained to predict these human preferences. Finally, the language model is fine-tuned using reinforcement learning to produce outputs that score highly according to the reward model. This process aligns the model's behaviour more closely with what humans actually find helpful, honest, and harmless — though it is far from perfect and remains an active area of research.

Prompt engineering emerged as a practical discipline once foundation models became capable enough that the way you phrase a request significantly affects the quality of the response. Effective prompting involves providing clear instructions, relevant context, examples of desired outputs, and structured formatting cues. Chain-of-thought prompting — asking the model to reason step by step — consistently improves performance on complex reasoning tasks. Few-shot prompting provides examples within the prompt to guide the model's behaviour without any additional training. While prompt engineering is often dismissed as a temporary skill that will become unnecessary as models improve, it reflects a deeper truth: communicating intent clearly to an AI system is a genuine skill that requires understanding both what the model can do and how it processes instructions.
keyPoints: Supervised learning maps labelled inputs to outputs and is the foundation of classification, regression, and prediction systems
Unsupervised learning discovers structure in unlabelled data, enabling clustering, dimensionality reduction, and generative modelling
Reinforcement learning trains agents through environmental interaction and reward signals, powering game-playing AI and language model alignment
The transformer architecture and its attention mechanism are the foundation of virtually all frontier AI systems since 2017
Embeddings convert diverse inputs — text, images, audio — into numerical vectors that capture semantic meaning and enable similarity comparisons
Training is the expensive process of learning parameters from data; inference is the cheap process of using a trained model on new inputs
Fine-tuning adapts pre-trained foundation models to specific domains, making expensive general-purpose training reusable across thousands of applications
RLHF aligns language models with human preferences by training on human-ranked comparisons of model outputs
Prompt engineering is a practical discipline for communicating intent effectively to AI systems, using techniques like chain-of-thought and few-shot examples
Transfer learning — the ability to apply knowledge from one domain to another — is what makes foundation models economically viable and widely applicable
furtherReading: 'Deep Learning' by Ian Goodfellow, Yoshua Bengio, and Aaron Courville — comprehensive technical reference for neural network architectures and training
'Attention Is All You Need' (Vaswani et al., 2017) — the original transformer paper that launched the current era of AI
Andrej Karpathy's neural network lecture series — accessible video explanations of deep learning fundamentals from first principles
'The Illustrated Transformer' by Jay Alammar — visual explanation of transformer architecture and attention mechanisms
Lilian Weng's blog (lilianweng.github.io) — detailed, well-referenced technical explanations of modern AI concepts
